title: Ethereum Fraud Analytics
text:
One dataset, one question, five lenses. This suite investigates what distinguishes fraudulent from legitimate Ethereum addresses using the Kaggle Ethereum Fraud Detection dataset — 9,841 addresses × 50 aggregated transaction features, 22.1% flagged as fraudulent — through exploratory analysis in Python and statistical inference and modelling in R, on a single canonical data preparation.
Guiding question: what patterns in transaction volume, frequency, timing and token types indicate illicit activity and differentiate fraudulent Ethereum addresses from legitimate ones?

title: Ethereum Fraud Analytics
text:
Headline findings

Volume and frequency are partially independent signals — Ether sent and transaction count correlate positively but only weakly-to-moderately (RQ1), so each carries information the other doesn’t.
Addresses operate at a characteristic value scale — average values received and sent are strongly positively correlated (RQ2).
Fraud has a rhythm — the average time between sent transactions differs significantly between fraudulent and legitimate addresses (RQ3): a behavioural signal independent of value.
Token ecosystems matter — Ether outflow differs across dominant sent-token types (RQ4), and the dominant received token type is significantly associated with fraud status (RQ5).
Two label-leakage artefacts uncovered — all 829 addresses lacking ERC20 aggregates are fraudulent, and two scam-airdrop token types are 100%-fraud label proxies (Ch. 5). Any predictive use of this dataset must be leakage-aware.
The moderation question — the regression chapters test whether fraud status changes the sent→balance relationship (Ch. 4) and whether token type changes the behaviour→fraud relationship (Ch. 5), with full diagnostics, effect sizes and model comparisons.

title: Ethereum Fraud Analytics
text:
Provenance & method notes
This suite consolidates four R analyses and one Python notebook originally produced as separate projects. All code is shown (folded) on every page; the site is fully reproducible with quarto render.

title: 5 · Predicting fraud
text: Chapter 3 established that which token dominates an address’s inflow is associated with its fraud status. This chapter turns from association to conditional prediction: can transaction behaviour — how much an address transacts, how much of that is token activity, and how quickly it sends — predict the likelihood of a fraud flag? And does the token ecosystem change what those behaviours mean? The goal is inferential understanding of the signals, not a deployable classifier; that distinction ends up mattering more here than in any other chapter.

title: 5 · Predicting fraud
section: The analysis sample — and what the missing data quietly revealed
text:
The analysis sample — and what the missing data quietly revealed


Code
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(broom)
library(car)
library(pROC)

trxs <- readRDS("data/eth_clean.rds")

lr <- trxs %>%
  filter(!is.na(total_erc20_tnxs)) %>%
  add_count(erc20_most_rec_token_type, name = "type_n") %>%
  mutate(
    token_rec  = ifelse(type_n < 40, "Low Count", erc20_most_rec_token_type),
    token_rec  = relevel(factor(token_rec), ref = "None"),
    tnx_log    = log10(total_transactions + 1),
    erc20_log  = log10(total_erc20_tnxs + 1),
    avgmin_log = log10(avg_min_between_sent_tnx + 1)
  )

n_excl  <- sum(is.na(trxs$total_erc20_tnxs))
excl_fr <- sum(trxs$flag[is.na(trxs$total_erc20_tnxs)])
cat(nrow(lr), "addresses in sample;", sum(lr$flag), "flagged",
    sprintf("(%.1f%%; majority-class baseline accuracy %.1f%%)",
            100 * mean(lr$flag), 100 * (1 - mean(lr$flag))))


9012 addresses in sample; 1350 flagged (15.0%; majority-class baseline accuracy 85.0%)


The model needs the ERC20 aggregates, so the 829 addresses lacking them are excluded — and that routine step surfaced something worth pausing on. The fraud share drops from 22.1% to 15.0%, which is a large move for a 8% exclusion, and the reason is stark: all 829 of the excluded addresses are flagged fraudulent (829/829). In other words, in this dataset “ERC20 data missing” perfectly predicts the label. The most plausible cause is mechanical — the fraud-address list appears to have been assembled separately and joined in, with the ERC20 aggregates never computed for part of that batch — which makes missingness an artefact of dataset construction, not a behaviour of fraudsters. The practical consequences: missingness must never be used as a feature on this data, and everything below speaks for token-active addresses only. Low-frequency token types (< 40 addresses) are pooled into a "Low Count" level so group estimates stay stable, and "None" — no dominant ERC20 inflow — is kept as the meaningful reference category.

Read on the ×10 scale, the three effects sketch a coherent behavioural fingerprint. A ×10 increase in ordinary transactions multiplies the fraud odds by 0.2 — sustained, plain on-chain activity is what legitimacy looks like. A ×10 increase in ERC20 token transactions multiplies the odds by 4.6 — the flagged population is token-transfer-heavy. And a ×10 longer average gap between sent transactions multiplies the odds by 0.83: quicker sending rhythms lean fraudulent, echoing the cadence difference found in Chapter 3. Fraud, in this data, looks token-heavy, plain-transaction-light, and fast. The model’s McFadden pseudo-R² is 0.12 and the VIFs below confirm the three signals are not proxies for one another.

title: 5 · Predicting fraud
text:
From scores to decisions
A logistic model outputs probabilities; a fraud screen must output decisions, and the gap between those two is where the operational honesty lives.


Code
roc1 <- roc(lr$flag, fitted(m1), quiet = TRUE)
plot(roc1, legacy.axes = TRUE, col = "#2C7FB8",
     main = sprintf("Behaviour-only model — AUC %.2f", auc(roc1)))





Model 1 discrimination. AUC summarises ranking quality across all possible thresholds — it says nothing about any particular operating point.






Code
pred1 <- factor(ifelse(fitted(m1) >= 0.5, "Fraudulent", "Legitimate"),
                levels = c("Legitimate", "Fraudulent"))
cm <- table(pred1, lr$flag_f)

data.frame(
  ` ` = c("Predicted: Legitimate", "Predicted: Fraudulent"),
  `Actual: Legitimate` = cm[, "Legitimate"],
  `Actual: Fraudulent` = cm[, "Fraudulent"],
  check.names = FALSE
) %>%
  knitr::kable(row.names = FALSE,
               caption = "Confusion matrix at the default 0.5 threshold.")

The matrix tells the story the accuracy number hides. At the default threshold the model catches 265 of the 1350 flagged addresses (sensitivity 20%) while raising only 68 false alarms — and still posts 87% accuracy, because with 85% of addresses legitimate, accuracy mostly rewards saying “legitimate”. The 0.5 threshold is a statistical default, not a policy: in a real fraud operation the threshold is set by the relative cost of a missed fraud versus an investigation, and lowering it here would trade a manageable rise in false alarms for a large gain in recall. The behavioural signals rank addresses usefully (AUC 0.71); what to do with the ranking is a business decision.

section: Model 2: does the token ecosystem change what behaviour means?

Model 2: does the token ecosystem change what behaviour means?
Chapter 3 found the dominant received token associated with fraud; the sharper question is whether it moderates the behavioural signals — whether the same pattern of activity means something different inside different token communities. Model 2 interacts all three behavioural predictors with the token factor.

Both models’ ROC curves. The jump is real in-sample — and the next paragraphs explain why it should not be taken at face value.





Reading the jump carefully
An AUC of 0.98 from a logistic regression should raise an eyebrow before it raises applause, and here the skepticism pays off. Part of the improvement is genuine segment structure — Chapter 3’s association result made that expected. But a decisive part of it is simpler: 2 token levels in this sample coincide perfectly with the fraud flag — Blockwell say NOTSAFU (n = 779) and GSENetwork (n = 80) are flagged fraudulent without exception. Both are known spam-airdrop tokens, and the likely mechanism runs through how the dataset was built: the fraud list appears to draw heavily on addresses swept up in those scam ecosystems, so receiving these tokens is partly a consequence of how the label was constructed rather than an independent behavioural signal. Combined with the missingness artefact found earlier, the message is consistent: this dataset rewards models that memorise its construction.
For anyone taking this analysis toward a production model, three practices follow naturally: validate out-of-time rather than in-sample, so construction artefacts stop paying; ablate token identity and check how much discrimination survives on behaviour alone (here, an AUC of 0.71); and treat token context as segment information whose meaning must be re-learned as scam ecosystems change, not as a stable feature. Read that way, Model 2 is best understood as strong evidence that token context carries signal — and a worked example of why a fraud analyst never deploys an in-sample 0.98.

title: 5 · Predicting fraud
text:
What this chapter established
Behaviour alone ranks addresses usefully — token-heavy, plain-transaction-light, fast-cadence profiles carry materially higher flag odds — and token context adds real but contaminated lift whose honest assessment requires validation this cross-sectional dataset cannot provide. The estimates speak for token-active addresses, against a label of undocumented provenance, in-sample only; the deployment-grade treatment of this problem — held-out protocols, imbalance handling, cost-sensitive thresholds — is the subject of the dedicated machine-learning project that this suite’s conclusions point toward.

title: 3 · Hypothesis testing
text:
Chapter 2 ended with patterns an analyst can see: heavy-tailed value distributions, timing differences between the flagged and legitimate populations, token activity that looks fraud-linked, and value features that move together. Eyes are not evidence, though — apparent patterns in a 9,841-address snapshot can be noise, scale artefacts, or both. This chapter subjects five of those observations to formal statistical testing.
The five research questions fall into three natural groups. Two are about how value and activity relate — whether busier addresses send more Ether (RQ1) and whether addresses operate at a characteristic value scale (RQ2). One is about behaviour over time — whether fraudulent and legitimate addresses transact on different rhythms (RQ3). And two are about token ecosystems — whether Ether outflow differs across dominant sent-token types (RQ4) and whether the dominant received token is associated with the fraud flag itself (RQ5). Each question gets the same discipline: hypotheses stated up front, assumptions checked against an explicit criterion, the appropriate test run — and a second estimator that does not share the first one’s assumptions, so that no conclusion in this chapter hinges on a distributional judgement call.

title: 3 · Hypothesis testing
text:
Ground rules for every test
Two decisions apply throughout. First, the working scale: every value and volume variable here is heavily right-tailed (Chapter 2 made that vivid), so tests that assume anything about distribution shape operate on log10(x + 1) scales, while rank-based checks use the raw values they are indifferent to. Second, the normality criterion, stated explicitly rather than judged by eye: a transformed variable is treated as approximately normal for large-sample purposes when its standardised skew and kurtosis stay within ±3.29 and no more than 1% of standardised scores exceed |3.29|. The helper below implements exactly that assessment, and a small plotting helper shows each distribution against its normal reference.


Code
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(car)

trxs <- readRDS("data/eth_clean.rds")

# ---- Normality assessment helper -------------------------------------------
# Standardised skew & kurtosis plus the share of |z| beyond 1.96 / 3.29 —
# the large-sample criteria described above, made explicit and reusable.
normality_summary <- function(x, label) {
  x <- x[is.finite(x)]
  n <- length(x); m <- mean(x); s <- sd(x)
  z <- (x - m) / s
  m2 <- mean((x - m)^2); m3 <- mean((x - m)^3); m4 <- mean((x - m)^4)
  skew <- m3 / m2^1.5
  kurt <- m4 / m2^2 - 3
  se_skew <- sqrt(6 * n * (n - 1) / ((n - 2) * (n + 1) * (n + 3)))
  se_kurt <- 2 * se_skew * sqrt((n^2 - 1) / ((n - 3) * (n + 5)))
  tibble(variable = label, n = n, mean = m, sd = s,
         skew = skew, skew_std = skew / se_skew,
         kurtosis = kurt, kurt_std = kurt / se_kurt,
         `%|z|>1.96` = 100 * mean(abs(z) > 1.96),
         `%|z|>3.29` = 100 * mean(abs(z) > 3.29))
}

# ---- Distribution plot helper ----------------------------------------------
dist_plot <- function(data, var, label) {
  ggplot(data, aes(x = .data[[var]])) +
    geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 40,
                   colour = "black", fill = "grey80") +
    stat_function(fun = dnorm, colour = "red",
                  args = list(mean = mean(data[[var]], na.rm = TRUE),
                              sd = sd(data[[var]], na.rm = TRUE))) +
    labs(x = label, y = "Density") +
    theme_minimal()
}

# Log-transformed working copies for the shape-sensitive tests.
trxs <- trxs %>%
  mutate(
    total_ether_sent_log    = log10(total_ether_sent + 1),
    total_transactions_log  = log10(total_transactions + 1),
    avg_val_received_log    = log10(avg_val_received + 1),
    avg_val_sent_log        = log10(avg_val_sent + 1),
    avg_min_sent_log        = log10(avg_min_between_sent_tnx + 1)
  )

title: 3 · Hypothesis testing
section: RQ1 — Do addresses that transact more also send more Ether?
text:
RQ1 — Do addresses that transact more also send more Ether?
Chapter 2 showed volume and frequency both exploding across orders of magnitude; the first question is whether they explode together. If they largely do, one is redundant given the other; if they only loosely do, each carries its own signal.

H0: there is no relationship between total Ether sent and total number of transactions per address.
Ha: there is a relationship between total Ether sent and total number of transactions per address.



Code
bind_rows(
  normality_summary(trxs$total_ether_sent_log,   "log10(total_ether_sent + 1)"),
  normality_summary(trxs$total_transactions_log, "log10(total_transactions + 1)")
) %>% knitr::kable(digits = 2,
                   caption = "Normality assessment of the transformed variables against the ±3.29 criterion.")

  geom_point(alpha = 0.15, size = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", colour = "red") +
  labs(title = "Outflow rises with activity — but loosely",

Each point is an address. The association is clearly positive — and clearly loose: at any activity level, outflow still spans several orders of magnitude.




Finding. A statistically significant positive association: Pearson r = 0.383 (p < 0.001) on the transformed scales, with Kendall’s τ = 0.217 on the raw ranks confirming direction and significance. The association is real but weak-to-moderate — transaction count alone is a poor proxy for Ether volume. That looseness is itself informative: volume- and frequency-based signals carry partially independent information about an address, a fact the regression chapters will lean on.

title: 3 · Hypothesis testing
section: RQ2 — Do high-value receivers also send high values?
text:
RQ2 — Do high-value receivers also send high values?
The second value question is about scale of operation: does an address that receives large average amounts also send large average amounts — retail behaviour staying retail, whale behaviour staying whale?

Addresses do operate at a characteristic value scale, which is exactly why the conditional questions of the regression chapters (what happens at a fixed activity level) are the interesting ones.

title: 3 · Hypothesis testing
section: RQ3 — Does sending cadence differ between fraudulent and legitimate addresses?
text:
RQ3 — Does sending cadence differ between fraudulent and legitimate addresses?
From value to time. Chapter 2’s behavioural sections suggested the two populations move to different rhythms; this is the formal version of that comparison. Only addresses with at least one sent transaction — a defined cadence — enter it.

H0: the average time between sent transactions does not differ between fraudulent and legitimate addresses.
Ha: the average time between sent transactions differs between fraudulent and legitimate addresses.



Code

Finding. Levene’s test indicates unequal variances (p < 0.001), so Welch’s t-test is the primary test: the cadence difference between groups is statistically significant (t = -23.67, p < 0.001; Cohen’s d = -0.73), and the Mann–Whitney robustness check agrees (rank-biserial r = 0.42). Fraudulent and legitimate addresses transact on measurably different rhythms — a genuinely behavioural fraud signal, independent of value, and one that returns as a predictor in Chapter 5.

title: 3 · Hypothesis testing
section: RQ4 — Does Ether outflow differ across dominant sent-token types?
text:
RQ4 — Does Ether outflow differ across dominant sent-token types?
From individual behaviour to ecosystems. If token communities differ in how much Ether their addresses move, “which token dominates an address” is context that later models should not ignore. Token types with fewer than 40 addresses are excluded (unstable group estimates), as is "None" — no ERC20 activity is a different phenomenon than which token dominates.

H0: total Ether sent does not differ across the ERC20 token types most frequently sent from addresses.
Ha: total Ether sent differs across the ERC20 token types most frequently sent from addresses.

title: 3 · Hypothesis testing
section: RQ5 — Is the dominant received token associated with fraud?
text:
RQ5 — Is the dominant received token associated with fraud?
The final question turns the token lens onto the label itself. Low-frequency token types (< 40 addresses) are aggregated into "Low Count" so that expected cell counts support the χ² approximation; "None" is retained as a meaningful category.

H0: there is no association between the most frequently received ERC20 token type and the fraud flag.
Ha: there is an association between the most frequently received ERC20 token type and the fraud flag.



Code
rq5 <- trxs %>%
  add_count(erc20_most_rec_token_type, name = "type_n") %>%
  mutate(token_rec = ifelse(type_n < 40, "Low Count", erc20_most_rec_token_type))

rq5_tab  <- table(rq5$token_rec, rq5$flag_f)
rq5_chi  <- chisq.test(rq5_tab)
rq5_cram <- sqrt(rq5_chi$statistic /
                 (sum(rq5_tab) * (min(dim(rq5_tab)) - 1)))   # Cramér's V
rq5_chi

What the tests established



All five hypotheses in one view. In every case the robustness check agreed with the primary test.

Five questions, five rejections of the null — and, more importantly for what follows, none of them fragile: every verdict was confirmed by an estimator that does not share the primary test’s assumptions. Two threads now lead onward. The behavioural and value findings (RQ1–3) set up Chapter 4’s conditional question — at a fixed activity level, what remains in an address’s balance, and does fraud shift it? And the token findings (RQ4–5) set up Chapter 5’s — do the behavioural signals predict the flag, and does token context change what they mean? Everything here is inference about associations; prediction, with all its extra honesty requirements, comes next.

title: 1 · Data & preparation
text: Every chapter in this suite asks a different question of the same 9,841 addresses — which makes the preparation of those addresses the one place where quiet inconsistency could poison everything downstream. A correlation tested on one cleaning of the data and a regression fitted on a subtly different one are not results about the same population. So preparation is treated here as a first-class analysis step: the raw file is loaded once, every cleaning decision is made once — stated and justified where a reader can disagree with it — and the result is exported as the single starting point for the statistical chapters. The Python exploration deliberately begins from the same raw file instead, walking the data by hand; that is where these decisions are earned rather than asserted.

title: 1 · Data & preparation
text:
Source
The Kaggle Ethereum Fraud Detection dataset: one row per Ethereum address, aggregating its transaction history into activity, value, timing and ERC20-token features, with FLAG = 1 marking addresses known to have engaged in fraudulent activity.


Code
library(dplyr)

trxs_raw <- read.csv("data/transaction_dataset.csv")
dim(trxs_raw)


[1] 9841   50

title: 1 · Data & preparation
text:
Canonical column names
read.csv mangles the original headers (spaces and punctuation become dots, a leading space becomes an X. prefix). Everything is normalised to snake_case once, and the one unwieldy name the analyses use constantly is shortened.


Code
nm <- tolower(names(trxs_raw))
nm <- gsub("\\.+", "_", nm)        # runs of dots -> single underscore
nm <- gsub("^x_|^_|_$", "", nm)    # strip artefact prefixes / stray underscores
names(trxs_raw) <- nm

trxs <- trxs_raw %>%
  rename(total_transactions = total_transactions_including_tnx_to_create_contract)

title: 1 · Data & preparation
text:
Cleaning decisions
Four decisions shape the analysis sample — each written down precisely so a reader can push back on it.
Fraud label. Kept both as the numeric flag (for modelling) and as a labelled factor flag_f (for tables and plots). The class balance is retained as-is — the imbalance is a property of the phenomenon, and chapters that are sensitive to it (the logistic regression in particular) address it explicitly rather than resample it away.
ERC20 token types. The two categorical token columns are polluted with placeholder values — "", "0", and whitespace variants — which all mean the same thing: no dominant ERC20 token. They are unified into an explicit "None" category rather than dropped, because “no ERC20 activity” is itself informative.
Missing ERC20 aggregates. 829 addresses have NA across the ERC20 aggregate columns. They are kept here; chapters drop them only where a model requires those variables, and say so. This quirk turns out to be more than a quirk — Chapter 5 returns to it with a finding worth the wait.
Negative balances. 978 addresses show a negative total_ether_balance, an artefact of how the aggregate was computed at extraction time rather than a possible state of an account. They too are kept here and handled where they matter — the linear-regression chapter, which excludes them with justification.


Code
clean_token <- function(x) {
  x <- trimws(as.character(x))
  ifelse(is.na(x) | x %in% c("", "0", "None"), "None", x)
}

trxs <- trxs %>%
  mutate(
    flag_f = factor(flag, levels = c(0, 1),
                    labels = c("Legitimate", "Fraudulent")),
    erc20_most_sent_token_type = clean_token(erc20_most_sent_token_type),
    erc20_most_rec_token_type  = clean_token(erc20_most_rec_token_type)
  )

text:
Export the canonical dataset


Code

The statistical chapters start from readRDS("data/eth_clean.rds") and inherit every decision above without repeating it. The Python exploration chapter intentionally does not: it works from the same raw file and performs its own cleaning in full view, because watching the data get cleaned — the missingness, the placeholder values, the tails — is where the decisions codified here stop looking arbitrary. Two tracks, one raw source, consistent pictures.

title: 2 · Exploring the fraud landscape
text:
A hands-on Python walk through the data: cleaning, distributions, behaviour, risk, and drill-down scenarios.
Chapter 1 fixed a canonical preparation for the statistical chapters; this chapter takes the complementary route and works through the same raw file by hand. Doing both is deliberate: before any hypothesis is stated or model fitted, an analyst should know the data’s texture — where values pile up and where they explode, what is missing and why that might be, how rare the thing being studied actually is. Those observations are what make the later choices (log scales, robustness checks, imbalance-aware evaluation) feel inevitable rather than arbitrary.
The data is the Kaggle Ethereum Fraud Detection dataset: one row per Ethereum address (9,841 in total, 50 features), aggregating each address’s transaction history — volumes, timings, counterparties, Ether values and ERC20 token activity — together with a FLAG marking addresses known to have engaged in fraud. Roughly one address in five is flagged, an imbalance that shapes how every later result in this suite should be read.
The exploration runs in three movements: cleaning and inspection (missingness, imputation, distributions, outliers, class balance), general investigation (transaction- and address-level patterns, then behaviour, risk and correlation analysis), and drill-down scenarios that chase specific fraud hypotheses through the data. It closes with what these patterns suggest — and what, at this stage, they cannot yet prove.

title: 2 · Exploring the fraud landscape
text:
General Exploration and Investigation
With the data cleaned and its shape understood, the investigation widens: how do transactions and addresses behave — and where do the flagged and legitimate populations part ways?

Transaction-based Investigation
This part focuses on specific columns for understanding transaction-related behaviour in the dataset and potential connection with (indicators) of fraudulent activity :
Distribution of Transaction Counts, highlights the transaction count distribution for fraudulent vs. legitimate addresses:
- Fraudulent accounts 'cluster' at lower (or higher transaction counts) as compared to legitimate ones.
- The shape of the distribution (skewness) and the KDE curve indicate (potentialy unusual) behavioral differences.
Avg Value Sent vs Received, visualises the relationship between ‘avg val sent’ and avg ‘val received’ for fraud vs. non-fraud groups
- Linear trend suggests that accounts generally maintain proportional sent/received values.
- Outliers (addresses with high 'avg val sent' but 'low avg val received') indicate unusual activity patterns.
Avg Minutes Between Sent Transactions: compares the average time gap between transactions for fraud and legitimate addresses.
- Shorter intervals might suggest automated behavior (for example bot accounts).
- Fraudulent accounts could have either very high or very low activity density, depending on their purpose.

Account Lifetime vs Total Transactions:

Risk Analysis
Transaction Ratio (tx_ratio): Measures imbalance between sent and received transactions, a high ratio could indicate suspicious behavior (accounts mainly used for outgoing transactions).
Activity Intensity (activity_intensity): Measures the frequency of sent transactions relative to the account’s active time. A high value could mean excessive or unusual activity.
Risk Score: Combines outlier indicators:
- Flags accounts with extreme values (above the 95th percentile) for:
- tx_ratio
- activity_intensity
- Contracts created


Code
#
# Risk analysis
#

# identify high-risk patterns
risk_factors = df.copy()
risk_factors['tx_ratio'] = df['sent tnx'] / (df['received tnx'] + 1)
risk_factors['activity_intensity'] = df['sent tnx'] / (df['time diff between first and last (mins)'] + 1)

# calculate risk scores based on calculated
risk_factors['risk_score'] = (
    (risk_factors['tx_ratio'] > risk_factors['tx_ratio'].quantile(0.95)).astype(int) +
    (risk_factors['activity_intensity'] > risk_factors['activity_intensity'].quantile(0.95)).astype(int) +
    (risk_factors['number of created contracts'] > risk_factors['number of created contracts'].quantile(0.95)).astype(int)
)

print("\n=== High Risk Pattern Analysis ===")
high_risk = risk_factors[risk_factors['risk_score'] >= 2]
print(f"Number of addresses with high-risk patterns: {len(high_risk)} ({len(high_risk)/len(df)*100:.2f}%)")

title: 2 · Exploring the fraud landscape
text:
What the exploration established
Read together, the three movements leave four durable observations. First, every value and volume variable is heavily right-tailed, which is why log scales are the working currency of every later chapter. Second, the label is imbalanced (~22% flagged), which means raw accuracy will flatter any classifier and evaluation must be imbalance-aware — a point Chapter 5 returns to with numbers. Third, the two populations differ behaviourally, not just in scale: most strikingly, fraudulent addresses operate within far narrower networks, interacting on average with ~12.5 unique senders and ~3.3 unique recipients versus ~35 and ~32 for legitimate addresses, alongside distinct timing and token-activity patterns. Fourth, the value features correlate in clusters, so the signals overlap — no single variable tells the story alone.
These are descriptive observations: patterns an analyst can see, not yet claims that survive scrutiny. Chapter 3 puts five of them under formal statistical testing, and Chapters 4 and 5 ask whether they predict — retained balance, and the fraud flag itself.

title: 4 · Predicting Ether balance
text:
An address’s balance is a behavioural quantity, not just an accounting one. Two addresses can move identical volumes of Ether and end up in very different places: one accumulates, the other passes everything through. In fraud analytics that distinction matters — pass-through behaviour is the signature of mule-like accounts — so this chapter asks a precise version of the question: conditional on how much an address sends and how often it transacts, does anything remain? And is that pattern different for fraudulent addresses — not just in level, but in shape?
Two linear models answer it: a main-effects model, and one that lets the sent→balance slope differ by fraud status.

title: 4 · Predicting Ether balance
text:
Setting up the analysis


Code
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(broom)
library(car)

trxs <- readRDS("data/eth_clean.rds")

mlr <- trxs %>%
  filter(is.finite(total_ether_balance), total_ether_balance >= 0) %>%
  mutate(
    balance_log = log10(total_ether_balance + 1),
    sent_log    = log10(total_ether_sent + 1),
    tnx_log     = log10(total_transactions + 1)
  )


Two preparation decisions shape everything downstream. First, 978 addresses carry a negative recorded balance — an artefact of how the aggregate was computed at extraction time rather than a possible state of an account — and are excluded, at the cost of about 10% of the sample (n = 8863 remains). Second, every continuous variable in this dataset is heavily right-tailed, so the analysis works on log10(x + 1) scales. That choice buys well-behaved models but changes how coefficients read: each coefficient describes what a ×10 change in a predictor does, multiplicatively, to (balance + 1) — a reading guide worth keeping in mind for every table below.

title: 4 · Predicting Ether balance
section: A first model: activity, outflow, and fraud status
text:
A first model: activity, outflow, and fraud status

m1 <- lm(balance_log ~ sent_log + tnx_log + flag_f, data = mlr)

tidy(m1, conf.int = TRUE) %>%

Three effects, all large and all interpretable. Holding the others fixed:

Transaction count builds balance. A ×10 increase in total transactions is associated with multiplying (balance + 1) by 3.2 — roughly +222%. Busier addresses hold more, which is what accumulation looks like.
Outflow drains it — even conditionally. At a fixed transaction count, a ×10 increase in total Ether sent is associated with a -48% change in (balance + 1). The sign is the substantive finding: once you control for how often an address transacts, sending more is pass-through, not throughput.
Fraudulent addresses retain less. At the same activity level, a flagged address is associated with -42% in (balance + 1) relative to a legitimate one — consistent with accounts built to move value on rather than to hold it.

Together the three predictors account for 35% of the variance in log-balance — a solid share for three aggregates, while leaving plenty of address-level behaviour unexplained.
To compare the predictors on a common scale:

ncv_p <- ncvTest(m1)$p
cooks_flagged <- sum(cooks.distance(m1) > 4 / nrow(mlr))


The diagnostics say: directionally trustworthy, precisely optimistic. A formal check confirms what the residual plot shows — non-constant variance (Breusch–Pagan p < 0.001) — which means classical standard errors are somewhat too confident. That caveat is harmless for the three main effects (|t| ≥ 14; their direction and significance are not in doubt) but it matters for borderline estimates, a point that becomes relevant in the next section. Influence checks flag 491 observations (5.5% of the sample) by the 4/n Cook’s-distance rule — genuine extreme-but-real addresses, retained rather than trimmed.

section: Does fraud bend the relationship, or just shift it?
text:
Does fraud bend the relationship, or just shift it?

Raw two-variable view for orientation: each line is an unadjusted straight-line fit of log-balance on log-outflow within a group (the model above additionally controls for transaction count). The dotted line marks the floor of the response — log10(balance + 1) cannot be negative — and where the data thin out at high outflow the unbounded linear fit crosses it: a visible reminder that a straight line is a global summary, not a constrained prediction.




The interaction estimate is -0.044 with p = 0.044 — under the conventional threshold, but only just, and this is exactly where two earlier observations converge: with n = 8,863, effects this small reach significance easily, and the heteroscedasticity noted above makes borderline p-values the least reliable numbers in the chapter.

Statistical significance and practical importance part ways here, and the comparison table is the referee: the interaction improves adjusted R² by 0.0002 and AIC by about 2 points. A slope difference that small changes no substantive conclusion, so Model 1 is the model to keep: fraud status shifts the level of retained balance clearly, but there is no meaningful evidence that it changes the shape of the outflow–balance relationship.

title: 4 · Predicting Ether balance
text:
What this chapter established
Balance behaves like a behavioural fingerprint: activity accumulates it, outflow drains it conditionally, and fraudulent addresses systematically retain less — about -42% in (balance + 1) at equal activity — with no practically meaningful interaction on top. The estimates describe cross-sectional aggregates on multiplicative (log) scales, exclude the ~10% of addresses with artefactual negative balances, and inherit the usual caution that heavy-tailed data place on precise intervals; none of those caveats touches the direction or substance of the three main effects.

title: 6 · Conclusions
section: What it means for fraud analytics
text:
What it means for fraud analytics
The picture that emerges is behavioural: fraud-relevant signal lives not only in how much value moves, but in when it moves (cadence), how it decomposes (volume vs frequency), and where it moves (token ecosystems) — and the token context plausibly moderates the behavioural signals rather than merely adding to them. That is exactly the structure production fraud systems encode as engineered features and feature crosses, which makes these inferential results a principled feature-selection argument for predictive modelling.

title: 6 · Conclusions
text:
Limitations
Cross-sectional aggregates rather than transaction-level flows; a fraud label of undocumented provenance; heavy-tailed variables analysed on log scales (multiplicative interpretations); multiple hypotheses on one dataset (results should be read as an integrated investigation, not independent discoveries); in-sample model assessment — deployment-grade validation belongs to a dedicated ML treatment; and two label-leakage artefacts (missing ERC20 aggregates and scam-airdrop token identities perfectly predicting the flag) that make naive predictive claims on this dataset untrustworthy — documented in Chapter 5.

title: 6 · Conclusions
text:
Where this goes next
The natural continuation is predictive: supervised learning with a proper held-out protocol, class-imbalance handling, and cost-sensitive threshold selection — the subject of a separate end-to-end project (phishing detection pipeline) in this portfolio — and, on the data side, transaction-level (rather than aggregate) features, where the analysis finding suggests the richest signal lives.
